_purpose: Regional migration trigger — Shenandoah Valley farm families moving west through the gaps into Kentucky and Tennessee (1750-1850), and secondarily Valley/Piedmont families moving to Richmond/Tidewater industry. Supplements the national scots_irish_frontier trigger with SPECIFIC Valley origin counties, specific mountain passes (Cumberland Gap, Pound Gap, the gaps of the Alleghenies), specific destination counties in Kentucky and Tennessee, and the distinctive Valley community — mixed Scots-Irish and German — that produced these migrants. This is not a generic frontier trigger. It is about what happened when the Valley filled up and the younger sons looked west.
display_name: Shenandoah Valley Westward Migration Through the Gaps
short_description: Between 1750 and 1850, thousands of Scots-Irish and German families who had settled the Shenandoah Valley of Virginia pushed west through the gaps of the Allegheny and Cumberland mountains into Kentucky and Tennessee. The Valley was the staging ground — families arrived via the Great Wagon Road from Pennsylvania, farmed a generation or two, and then sent younger sons through Cumberland Gap or the New River route into the trans-Appalachian country. By 1800, entire Valley kinship networks had reconstituted themselves along the Holston, Clinch, Kentucky, and Cumberland rivers.
estimated_scale: Tens of thousands of families over a century. Augusta County alone contributed enough westward migrants that its court records are essential sources for Kentucky and Tennessee genealogy. Rockbridge, Rockingham, and Shenandoah counties show similar outflows. The 1790-1820 period was the peak: Kentucky's population grew from 73,677 (1790) to 564,317 (1820), with the Shenandoah Valley as a primary feeder.
coercion_note: This migration was genuinely voluntary in most cases — driven by ambition for land rather than desperation. However, younger sons who inherited nothing under partible inheritance had limited options: stay and work as tenants on a brother's land, or go west. For them, 'voluntary' meant 'the best of a constrained set of choices.' For the enslaved people brought west by Valley slaveholders, the migration was entirely involuntary.
regional_scope: Shenandoah Valley of Virginia (Augusta, Rockbridge, Rockingham, Shenandoah, Frederick, Page, Warren counties) west through the Allegheny passes and Cumberland Gap into the Holston-Clinch valley of southwestern Virginia, then into Kentucky (Bluegrass region, eastern Kentucky mountains) and Tennessee (East Tennessee valley, Middle Tennessee basin).

phases.description: Valley men — hunters and land scouts — pushed through the gaps to explore the trans-Appalachian country. The Long Hunters (so called because they stayed out for months or years) brought back reports of rich land along the Kentucky and Cumberland rivers. Daniel Boone, who operated out of the Yadkin Valley but traveled the same routes, was the most famous but far from the only one. Valley families like the Prestons, Campbells, and Breckinridges organized land companies and surveys. The Watauga Settlement (1769) and Harrodsburg (1774) were the first permanent settlements by Valley-origin families west of the Alleghenies. The Revolutionary War interrupted but did not stop the movement — militia service in the west (King's Mountain 1780, the Illinois campaigns) exposed Valley soldiers to western lands and generated bounty-land claims that pulled them back after the war.

After the Revolution, the westward movement became a flood. Virginia's military bounty lands in Kentucky drew veterans and their families. The opening of Cumberland Gap as a wagon road (the Wilderness Road, improved by 1796) made family migration feasible — before that, only men on horseback or on foot could traverse the trail. Valley families sold their Virginia land, loaded wagons, and headed through the gaps in organized parties of kin and neighbors. Augusta County court records from this period are full of land sales and powers of attorney documenting departure. The Kentucky and Tennessee county courts simultaneously fill with deeds, marriage bonds, and tax lists recording the same families' arrival. This was the peak period — more Valley families crossed the mountains between 1790 and 1810 than in any other two decades.

By 1810, the best Kentucky and Tennessee land was claimed. Late Valley migrants pushed further — into southern Indiana, central Missouri, and eventually the Ozarks. Some Valley families, particularly Germans, turned east instead of west, moving to Richmond, Lynchburg, and Staunton for trade and manufacturing opportunities. The westward movement continued but at reduced volume and toward more distant destinations. The Valley itself matured — towns like Staunton, Lexington, Harrisonburg, and Winchester grew, the Virginia Central Railroad arrived in the 1850s, and the Valley's identity shifted from frontier staging ground to established agricultural region. Families who stayed prospered on the rich limestone soils; families who left carried Valley surnames, Valley church affiliations, and Valley inheritance disputes into a dozen western states.

push_factors.factor: Population pressure and land subdivision in the Valley
push_factors.description: The Shenandoah Valley filled rapidly after initial Scots-Irish and German settlement in the 1730s-1740s. By the 1770s, good Valley land was claimed. Virginia's inheritance laws divided land among heirs, and after two or three generations of subdivision, Valley farms were too small for the large families they produced. A 400-acre tract granted to a grandfather in 1740 might be split four ways by 1780 and sixteen ways by 1820. Younger sons — the ones who inherited the smallest, least productive parcels — were the primary westward migrants. They did not leave because the Valley was bad. They left because it was full.
push_factors.regional_scope: Shenandoah Valley — Augusta, Rockbridge, Rockingham, Shenandoah, Frederick counties
push_factors.narrative_hook: The {surname} family had farmed in {county} since {ancestor_name}'s grandfather took up the patent in the {decade}s. By {year}, the old tract had been divided among so many heirs that {ancestor_name}'s share wouldn't support a family. The Valley was rich land — but there wasn't enough of it anymore. The country beyond the mountains had land to spare.

push_factors.factor: Younger sons with no viable inheritance
push_factors.description: Partible inheritance under Virginia law meant that all sons received a share, but in practice, the eldest son often received the homeplace and the best land, while younger sons received marginal parcels or cash settlements. A younger son with 50 acres of hillside when his brother had 200 acres of bottomland faced a choice: work as a tenant, learn a trade, or go west. The westward migration was disproportionately a younger-son migration. Court records show this pattern clearly — eldest sons appearing on Valley tax lists for decades, younger brothers appearing once or twice and then vanishing, reappearing on Kentucky or Tennessee tax lists a year or two later.
push_factors.narrative_hook: {ancestor_name} was the {birth_order} of {sibling_count} sons. When the old man's estate was divided, {eldest_brother} got the homeplace on {creek_name} in {county}. {ancestor_name} got {acres} acres of ridge land that wouldn't grow enough corn to feed a mule. The choice was clear enough: stay and hire out to {eldest_brother}, or head for the Gap.

push_factors.factor: Soil depletion from tobacco cultivation without rotation
push_factors.description: While the Valley's limestone soils were far more resilient than coastal plain soils, continuous tobacco and grain cultivation without systematic rotation depleted even the best bottomland. German farmers practiced better soil management than their Scots-Irish neighbors (crop rotation, manuring, cover crops), which is one reason German families were more likely to stay while Scots-Irish families moved on. By the late 1700s, some Valley farms that had been in cultivation for two generations showed reduced yields, adding economic pressure to the demographic pressure of subdivision.
push_factors.regional_scope: Shenandoah Valley, particularly areas of continuous tobacco cultivation
push_factors.narrative_hook: The land on {creek_name} had been planted in tobacco since {ancestor_name}'s grandfather's time. The first crops were abundant; the Valley soil was deep and rich. By {year}, the fields were tired — yields half what they had been a generation before. The German neighbors rotated their crops and spread manure. The {surname} family planted tobacco again and talked about Kentucky.

push_factors.factor: Indian wars disrupted frontier Valley settlements
push_factors.description: The French and Indian War (1754-1763) and Dunmore's War (1774) devastated the western Valley and its frontier extensions. Shawnee and Cherokee raids burned farms, killed settlers, and drove families into forts for months at a time. The Kerr's Creek massacre (1759), the Big Calf Pasture attack (1763), and dozens of smaller raids depopulated parts of Augusta and Bath counties. Paradoxically, the Indian wars both delayed and accelerated westward migration: they delayed it during active conflict, but the military campaigns (especially the Point Pleasant campaign of 1774 and the Revolutionary War campaigns in Kentucky) exposed Valley men to western lands and generated military bounty-land claims that drew them west after the fighting ended.
push_factors.regional_scope: Western Shenandoah Valley frontier — Augusta, Bath, Greenbrier (now WV), Montgomery counties
push_factors.narrative_hook: {ancestor_name} fought at Point Pleasant in 1774, marching through some of the richest country {pronoun_subject} had ever seen — bottomland along the Ohio that made the Valley farm look like a stone pile. When the war ended and Virginia started granting bounty land in Kentucky, {ancestor_name} knew exactly where {pronoun_subject} wanted to go.

pull_factors.factor: Cheap and abundant land in Kentucky and Tennessee
pull_factors.description: Kentucky and Tennessee offered what the Valley no longer had: vast tracts of unclaimed (by Europeans) land at prices far below Valley land values, or free through military bounty warrants, preemption claims, and simple occupation. Kentucky's Bluegrass region had extraordinary soil — deep limestone-based loam that produced corn, hemp, and livestock in abundance. Valley families could sell a 100-acre Valley farm and buy 500 or 1,000 acres in Kentucky. The promise was not just land but dynasties — enough land to provide for sons and grandsons without the subdivision that had squeezed the Valley.
pull_factors.narrative_hook: In {county} County, Virginia, land sold for ${va_price} an acre when you could find any for sale. Across the mountains in Kentucky, {ancestor_name} could claim {acres} acres for the cost of a survey and a filing fee. The arithmetic was simple. The Valley was behind {pronoun_object}. The future was through the Gap.

pull_factors.factor: Similar mountain and valley terrain familiar to Valley settlers
pull_factors.description: The country west of the Alleghenies — the Holston and Clinch valleys of southwestern Virginia, the Kentucky Bluegrass, the Great Valley of East Tennessee — was geographically similar to the Shenandoah Valley. Valley settlers knew how to farm limestone valleys, how to navigate mountain terrain, and how to live at the edge of the frontier. Unlike coastal planters who would have been lost in the mountains, Valley Scots-Irish and Germans were culturally and practically adapted to exactly the kind of country they were entering. This familiarity reduced the psychological barrier to migration.
pull_factors.narrative_hook: The country along the {river_name} in {destination_county} looked like home — the same kind of limestone valley, the same kind of hardwood ridges, the same kind of creek bottoms where a man could make a farm. {ancestor_name} had crossed the mountains to find a place that was just like the place left behind, except bigger and emptier.

pull_factors.factor: Kinship networks already established at western destinations
pull_factors.description: Once the first Valley families crossed the mountains, they became anchors for chain migration. Letters back to Augusta or Rockbridge counties reported on land availability, soil quality, and the location of kin. Families traveled in groups organized by kinship and neighborhood — entire Presbyterian congregations sometimes relocated together. In Kentucky and Tennessee, Valley families clustered by origin county: Augusta County families in one drainage, Rockbridge families in another, forming communities that replicated Valley social structures in a new landscape. The kinship chain meant that westward migration was not a leap into the unknown but a move to a place where cousins were already waiting.
pull_factors.narrative_hook: {relative_name} had crossed over to Kentucky the year before and written back that the land on {creek_name} was better than anything left in {county} County. There were {family_count} families from {county} already settled within a day's ride. When {ancestor_name} loaded the wagon, {pronoun_subject} wasn't heading into the wilderness. {pronoun_subject} was heading to where the family already was.

pull_factors.factor: Military bounty-land warrants from the Revolution
pull_factors.description: Virginia granted military bounty land in Kentucky to Revolutionary War veterans based on rank and length of service. A private received 100 acres; a colonel received 5,000 or more. Many veterans sold their warrants to speculators, but many others — especially Valley men who had already seen the western country during their service — used them to establish western farms. Bounty-land warrants were also inherited by widows and heirs, pulling the next generation west. The Virginia Military District in Kentucky (between the Scioto and Little Miami rivers in what is now Ohio) was another destination for Virginia bounty-land holders.
pull_factors.narrative_hook: {ancestor_name} served {years} years in the Virginia Line and came home to {county} County with a bounty warrant for {acres} acres of Kentucky land. The Valley farm was too small for the family. The Kentucky warrant was a way out — not charity, but pay earned in blood. By {year}, {ancestor_name} had surveyed the claim and started building.

pull_factors.factor: Cumberland Gap opened a feasible wagon route west
pull_factors.description: Cumberland Gap — the great natural passage through the Cumberland Mountains at the junction of Virginia, Kentucky, and Tennessee — was the keyhole that unlocked the trans-Appalachian frontier. Daniel Boone and others blazed the Wilderness Road through the Gap in 1775. Initially a narrow trace passable only on foot or horseback, it was gradually improved until wagons could pass by 1796. For Valley families, the Gap was the practical gateway: south down the Valley, through the gaps at the headwaters of the Holston and Clinch rivers, then through Cumberland Gap into Kentucky. Estimated 200,000-300,000 people passed through Cumberland Gap between 1775 and 1810.
pull_factors.narrative_hook: The Gap was narrow — barely wide enough for a wagon between the rock walls — and the road through it was a disaster of mud, roots, and loose stone. But beyond it was Kentucky. {ancestor_name} brought the family through Cumberland Gap in {year}, one wagon among hundreds that season, part of a stream of Valley families pouring through the mountains toward land they could call their own.

transportation.description: Valley-to-west migration used a combination of the Great Wagon Road (south through the Valley), the Wilderness Road (through Cumberland Gap into Kentucky), and the New River/Kanawha route (northwest through the Alleghenies into the Ohio Valley). Families traveled by wagon when roads permitted, by pack horse on narrow trails, and on foot through the worst terrain. River transport on the Holston, Clinch, and Kentucky rivers supplemented overland travel for families and goods moving deeper into the western country. The journey from the central Valley (Augusta County) to central Kentucky (Bluegrass region) was roughly 300-400 miles and took 3-6 weeks by wagon, depending on weather, road conditions, and the size of the traveling party.
transportation.route_notes: The Great Wagon Road connected the Valley to its southern and southwestern extensions. Families heading for Kentucky or Tennessee left the Wagon Road at various points: some turned west through Rockfish Gap or the James River gaps into the Greenbrier country; others continued south to the Holston valley and turned west through Moccasin Gap or southwest through Cumberland Gap. The Wilderness Road (Boone's Trace) through Cumberland Gap was the primary route into central Kentucky. The New River route, following the New River northwest through the Alleghenies, was the primary route for families heading to the Kanawha and Ohio valleys.
transportation.what_they_carried: Household goods loaded in Conestoga or farm wagons: iron cookware, bedding, tools (axes, hoes, plows if space permitted), seed corn, flax seed, rifles and ammunition, salt, a few head of livestock driven alongside. Family Bible with the record of births, marriages, and deaths. Some carried disassembled looms or spinning wheels. Wealthier families brought enslaved people.
transportation.what_they_left_behind: Valley homesteads — stone or log houses on limestone land that had been in the family for one or two generations. Presbyterian or German Reformed church communities. The county courthouse where their deeds, marriages, and wills were recorded. Family cemeteries in the Valley churchyards. For the Scots-Irish, it was the second or third time the family had left an established home — Ulster to Pennsylvania, Pennsylvania to the Valley, now Valley to Kentucky. For the Germans, it was often the first major displacement since arriving in America.
transportation.cost_of_travel: Variable. A wagon, team, and supplies for the journey cost $50-$150 depending on what the family already owned. The land itself was cheap — $0.10 to $2.00 per acre depending on location, quality, and era. Military bounty warrants were free to the veteran but valuable on the secondary market ($5-$50 depending on size and location). The real cost was time — 3-6 weeks on the road was 3-6 weeks not farming.
transportation.seasonal_departure_pattern: Departures concentrated in late winter and early spring (February-April) — after winter stores were consumed but before spring planting committed the family to another year in the Valley. Families aimed to arrive in Kentucky or Tennessee in time to plant a first crop. Fall departures (September-October) were less common but occurred, with families wintering at intermediate stops along the route.

record_implications.description: Augusta County, Virginia was the mother county for much of the westward migration. Its deed books, will books, order books (court minutes), and marriage bonds document families before they crossed the mountains. A deed of sale in Augusta County followed by disappearance from the tax lists is the classic signature of westward departure. Powers of attorney — authorizing someone to sell land or collect debts on behalf of a person now in Kentucky — document the migration in real time.
record_implications.where_to_find: Augusta County Courthouse, Staunton, VA. Library of Virginia, Richmond. FamilySearch microfilm. Chalkley's 'Chronicles of the Scotch-Irish Settlement in Virginia' (3 volumes, published 1912) abstracts Augusta County records through 1800.

record_implications.description: Virginia land patents document the initial Valley settlement. Military bounty-land warrants document the grants that pulled veterans west. Treasury warrants and preemption certificates document other land acquisitions. The land chain — patent in Augusta County, deed of sale, then a new patent or entry in Kentucky — traces the family's movement across the mountains.
record_implications.where_to_find: Virginia Land Office records at the Library of Virginia. Kentucky Land Office records at the Kentucky Secretary of State's office and the Kentucky Department for Libraries and Archives, Frankfort. Published in Jillson's 'Old Kentucky Entries and Deeds' and 'The Kentucky Land Grants.'

record_implications.description: Presbyterian session minutes document membership, baptisms, marriages, discipline, and letters of dismission. A letter of dismission from Tinkling Spring or Timber Ridge Presbyterian Church in Augusta County to a Kentucky or Tennessee congregation documents the family's migration with dates and destination. Session books also record moral discipline proceedings that provide biographical detail unavailable elsewhere.
record_implications.where_to_find: Presbyterian Historical Society, Philadelphia. Individual congregation records (Tinkling Spring, Timber Ridge, Falling Spring, Bethel, New Providence). Washington and Lee University Special Collections. Union Theological Seminary, Richmond.

record_implications.description: Revolutionary War service records, pension applications, and bounty-land warrant files document Valley men's military service and subsequent claims for western land. Pension applications filed in Kentucky or Tennessee by veterans who served in Virginia units are particularly valuable — they include detailed biographical narratives describing migration routes, dates, and family circumstances.
record_implications.where_to_find: National Archives (NARA), Washington, DC — Record Group 15 (Veterans Administration pension files), Record Group 49 (General Land Office bounty-land warrant files). Published abstracts in various state series. Fold3 and Ancestry.com have digitized many pension files.

record_implications.description: Lyman C. Draper spent decades (1840s-1880s) collecting documents and interviewing elderly frontier settlers and their children. His manuscript collection — over 500 volumes — is the single most important primary source for Valley-to-Kentucky/Tennessee migration. Draper's informants described specific migration routes, traveling parties, settlement dates, and family connections in detail that no other source preserves.
record_implications.where_to_find: Wisconsin Historical Society, Madison, WI. Microfilm widely available through interlibrary loan. Calendar (index) published by the Wisconsin Historical Society. Selected documents published in various state historical society journals.

record_implications.description: The genealogical fingerprint: a family appearing on the 1790 Virginia census (or earlier tax lists) in a Valley county, then appearing on the 1800 or 1810 Kentucky or Tennessee census in a western county. Because the 1790 census is the first federal census and it survives for Virginia, it provides a baseline for tracking families who subsequently moved west. Tax lists (Virginia personal property tax, Kentucky tax lists) fill gaps between census years.
record_implications.where_to_find: Federal census records at NARA and on Ancestry.com/FamilySearch. Virginia tax lists at the Library of Virginia. Kentucky tax lists at the Kentucky Department for Libraries and Archives. Published in various county history and genealogy compilations.

counter_narratives: Not everyone went west. Many Valley families stayed and prospered. The families who remained on Valley limestone land built one of the most productive agricultural regions in the South — the 'breadbasket of the Confederacy' during the Civil War. German families in particular were more likely to stay than Scots-Irish families, investing in soil improvement and diversified farming rather than chasing new land. The westward migration narrative overemphasizes departure and underemphasizes the families who chose to stay and build.

The Valley's enslaved population migrated west involuntarily. Valley slaveholders brought enslaved people through Cumberland Gap to clear land and build farms in Kentucky and Tennessee. The 'pioneer' narrative of Valley families seeking opportunity erases the coerced labor that made western farms viable. Augusta County had a significant enslaved population (20-25% by 1790), and the westward movement of these families is documented in estate inventories, bills of sale, and occasionally in the travel narratives themselves.

The Shawnee, Cherokee, and other Native peoples whose land was being occupied did not experience this migration as a story of opportunity. The Valley itself had been Iroquois, Shawnee, and Cherokee territory. Kentucky was contested hunting ground. The families who 'settled' the trans-Appalachian frontier did so by displacing Indigenous peoples through a combination of warfare, treaty fraud, and continuous encroachment. Every Valley family's 'new beginning' in Kentucky was someone else's dispossession.

Not all westward migrants were poor younger sons. Some were speculators and gentry — men like William Preston, James Patton, and the Breckinridge family — who used political connections, land warrants, and surveying contracts to assemble massive western landholdings. The migration had a class dimension: wealthy families accumulated land through legal and political maneuvering; poorer families squatted on land they could not afford to patent. The frontier was not as egalitarian as its mythology suggests.
narrative_hooks: The {surname} family had come down the Great Wagon Road from Pennsylvania and settled in {county} County in the {decade}s. Two generations later, the Valley was full and {ancestor_name} was looking west. Through Cumberland Gap, the country opened up — land enough for sons and grandsons, if a family was willing to cross the mountains to get it.

In {year}, {ancestor_name} sold the {county} County farm — {acres} acres that had been in the family since {settlement_year} — and loaded the wagon for Kentucky. The deed of sale is in the {county} County courthouse. The next record of the {surname} name is a land entry in {destination_county} County, Kentucky, filed {months} later. Between those two documents is a journey through Cumberland Gap that changed the family's world.

When {ancestor_name} came through the Gap in {year}, there were already {surname} cousins on the other side — {relative_name} had crossed over {years} years earlier and written back that the land along {creek_name} was good. The family was not scattering. It was transplanting itself — Presbyterian church, kinship network, and all — from one limestone valley to another.

{ancestor_name} fought in the Revolution with the Augusta County militia and came home with a bounty warrant for {acres} acres of Kentucky land. The Valley farm was spoken for — {eldest_brother} had the homeplace. The warrant was {ancestor_name}'s inheritance, written in gunpowder and signed by the Commonwealth of Virginia. By {year}, {pronoun_subject} had surveyed the claim and was building a cabin on {creek_name}.

source_references.citation: Hofstra, Warren R. The Planting of New Virginia: Settlement and Landscape in the Shenandoah Valley. Baltimore: Johns Hopkins University Press, 2004.
source_references.relevance: Definitive study of Shenandoah Valley settlement patterns, land distribution, and the ethnic composition (Scots-Irish and German) of Valley communities. Essential for understanding the origin community.

source_references.citation: Fischer, David Hackett. Albion's Seed: Four British Folkways in America. New York: Oxford University Press, 1989.
source_references.relevance: Chapter on 'Borderers' (Scots-Irish) documents the folkways, kinship patterns, and frontier settlement culture that Valley families carried west through the gaps.

source_references.citation: Leyburn, James G. The Scotch-Irish: A Social History. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1962.
source_references.relevance: Standard history of the Scots-Irish in America, documenting the migration from Ulster through Pennsylvania to the Valley and beyond.

source_references.citation: Chalkley, Lyman. Chronicles of the Scotch-Irish Settlement in Virginia, Extracted from the Original Court Records of Augusta County, 1745-1800. 3 vols. Rosslyn, VA: Commonwealth Printing Co., 1912.
source_references.relevance: Primary source abstracts from Augusta County court records documenting the Valley's Scots-Irish families — their land transactions, legal disputes, estate settlements, and departures for the west. Indispensable for genealogical research.

source_references.citation: Kegley, F.B. Kegley's Virginia Frontier: The Beginning of the Southwest, the Roanoke of Colonial Days, 1740-1783. Roanoke: Southwest Virginia Historical Society, 1938.
source_references.relevance: Documents the southwestern Virginia frontier through which Valley families passed en route to Kentucky and Tennessee. Identifies specific families, routes, and settlements.

source_references.citation: Drake, Daniel. Pioneer Life in Kentucky, 1785-1800. Edited by Emmet F. Horine. New York: Henry Schuman, 1948. Reprinted Lexington: University Press of Kentucky, 2001.
source_references.relevance: First-person account of frontier Kentucky settlement by a physician who grew up among Valley-origin families in the Bluegrass. Describes the material conditions, social networks, and daily life of transplanted Valley communities.

source_references.citation: Friend, Craig Thompson, ed. The Buzzel About Kentuck: Settling the Promised Land. Lexington: University Press of Kentucky, 1999.
source_references.relevance: Essay collection examining Kentucky settlement from multiple perspectives — land speculators, settlers, enslaved people, and Native Americans. Complicates the simple 'pioneer opportunity' narrative.

source_references.citation: Wust, Klaus. The Virginia Germans. Charlottesville: University Press of Virginia, 1969.
source_references.relevance: Documents the German communities of the Shenandoah Valley — their farming practices, religious institutions, and relationship to the Scots-Irish settlers with whom they shared the Valley.

source_references.citation: Draper Manuscripts. Wisconsin Historical Society, Madison, WI. Over 500 volumes of original documents and interviews relating to trans-Appalachian frontier settlement.
source_references.relevance: The largest primary source collection for Valley-to-Kentucky/Tennessee migration. Draper's interviews with frontier settlers and their descendants provide migration narratives unavailable in any other source.
